* China (*de facto*, as *Putonghua*)[[5]](#cite_note-Adamson_&_Feng-5) * Taiwan (*de facto*, as [Taiwanese Mandarin](/wiki/Taiwanese_Mandarin "Taiwanese Mandarin")[[6]](#cite_note-6))[[a]](#cite_note-8) * Singapore (as [Mandarin](/wiki/Singaporean_Mandarin "Singaporean Mandarin")) * United Nations (as Chinese) * [Shanghai Cooperation Organisation](/wiki/Shanghai_Cooperation_Organisation "Shanghai Cooperation Organisation")

* + [China](/wiki/China "China"): [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China")[[b]](#cite_note-9)   + [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"): [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Education (Taiwan)")   + [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"): [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Education (Malaysia)") and [Chinese Language Standardisation Council](/wiki/Chinese_Language_Standardisation_Council_of_Malaysia "Chinese Language Standardisation Council of Malaysia")   + [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"): [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Singapore) "Ministry of Education (Singapore)") [Promote Mandarin Council](/wiki/Promote_Mandarin_Council "Promote Mandarin Council")

For an introductory guide on IPA symbols, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). | |

([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 现代标准汉语; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 現代標準漢語; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Xiàndài biāozhǔn hànyǔ*), often colloquially called **Mandarin Chinese**,[[8]](#cite_note-Weng,_Jeffrey_2018_611–633-10) is the modern [standardized](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") form of the [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") language. It is the national [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of [China](/wiki/Languages_of_China "Languages of China"), one of the official languages of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations_languages "United Nations languages") and of [Singapore](/wiki/Languages_of_Singapore "Languages of Singapore"), and one of the national languages of [Taiwan](/wiki/Languages_of_Taiwan "Languages of Taiwan"). It is largely based on the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect"). Standard Chinese is a [pluricentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language") with local standards in mainland China, Taiwan and Singapore that mainly differ in their [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley1992307-11)
is used to distinguish it from older forms.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMair2013737-20)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoblin2000537-22)

**Standard Chinese** ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 现代标准汉语; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 現代標準漢語; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Xiàndài biāozhǔn hànyǔ*), often colloquially called **Mandarin Chinese**,[[8]](#cite_note-Weng,_Jeffrey_2018_611–633-10) is the modern [standardized](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") form of the [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") language. It is the national [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of [China](/wiki/Languages_of_China "Languages of China"), one of the official languages of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations_languages "United Nations languages") and of [Singapore](/wiki/Languages_of_Singapore "Languages of Singapore"), and one of the national languages of [Taiwan](/wiki/Languages_of_Taiwan "Languages of Taiwan"). It is largely based on the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect"). Standard Chinese is a [pluricentric language](/wiki/Pluricentric_language "Pluricentric language") with local standards in mainland China, Taiwan and Singapore that mainly differ in their [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon").[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley1992307-11)

Like other [Sinitic languages](/wiki/Sinitic_languages "Sinitic languages"), Standard Chinese is a [tonal language](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") with [topic-prominent](/wiki/Topic-prominent_language "Topic-prominent language") organization and [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") (SVO) word order. Compared with southern varieties, the language has fewer vowels, final consonants and tones, but more initial consonants. It is an [analytic language](/wiki/Analytic_language "Analytic language"), albeit with many [compound words](/wiki/Compound_word "Compound word").

## Naming

### In English

Among linguists, Standard Chinese has been referred to as **Standard Northern Mandarin**[[10]](#cite_note-John_Rohsenow_2004-12)[[11]](#cite_note-Yunyun_Ran_2016-13)[[12]](#cite_note-David_Bradley_2008-14) or **Standard Beijing Mandarin**.[[13]](#cite_note-Jeff_Siegel_2003-15)[[14]](#cite_note-Ying-Chuan_Chen2013-16) It is colloquially referred to as simply **Mandarin**,[[8]](#cite_note-Weng,_Jeffrey_2018_611–633-10) though this term may also refer to the [Mandarin dialect group](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") as a whole, or the late imperial [form used as a lingua franca](/wiki/Mandarin_(late_imperial_lingua_franca) "Mandarin (late imperial lingua franca)").[[15]](#cite_note-Robert_M_Sanders_1987-17)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988136-18)[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[8]](#cite_note-Weng,_Jeffrey_2018_611–633-10) "Mandarin" is a translation of *Guanhua* (官話; 官话; 'bureaucrat speech'),[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMair2013737-20) which referred to the late imperial lingua franca.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMair199111–12-21) The term **Modern Standard Mandarin** is used to distinguish it from older forms.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMair2013737-20)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoblin2000537-22)

### In Chinese

Among linguists, the language is referred to as Modern Standard Chinese ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 现代标准汉语; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 現代標準漢語; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Xiàndài biāozhǔn hànyǔ*) or Modern Standard Mandarin ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 现代标准官话; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 現代標準官話; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Xiàndài biāozhǔn guānhuà*). For both terms, the word Modern (现代; 現代; *Xiàndài*) is sometimes omitted.

#### *Guoyu*

The word *Guoyu* (国语; 國語; 'national language')[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMair2013737-20) was initially used during the late [Qing dynasty](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") to refer to the [Manchu language](/wiki/Manchu_language "Manchu language"). The 1655 *[Memoir of Qing Dynasty](/w/index.php?title=Memoir_of_Qing_Dynasty&action=edit&redlink=1 "Memoir of Qing Dynasty (page does not exist)"), Volume: Emperor [Nurhaci](/wiki/Nurhaci "Nurhaci")* (清太祖實錄) says: "(In 1631) as Manchu ministers do not comprehend the Han language, each ministry shall create a new position to be filled up by [Han](/wiki/Han_people "Han people") official who can comprehend the national language."[[21]](#cite_note-23) However, the sense of *Guoyu* as a specific language variety promoted for general use by the citizenry was originally borrowed from Japan in the early 20th century. In 1902, the Japanese Diet had formed the [National Language Research Council](/w/index.php?title=National_Language_Research_Council&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Language Research Council (page does not exist)") to standardize a form of the Japanese language dubbed *kokugo* (国語).[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETam202076-24) Reformers in the Qing bureaucracy took inspiration and borrowed the term into Chinese, and in 1909 the Qing education ministry officially proclaimed [imperial Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_(late_imperial_lingua_franca) "Mandarin (late imperial lingua franca)") to be the national language.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988133–134-25)

In Taiwan, *Guoyu* is the colloquial term for Standard Chinese. In 2017 and 2018, the Taiwanese government introduced two laws explicitly recognizing the indigenous [Formosan languages](/wiki/Formosan_languages "Formosan languages")[[24]](#cite_note-26)[[25]](#cite_note-Wang_Baijian2018-27) and [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese")[[26]](#cite_note-28)[[25]](#cite_note-Wang_Baijian2018-27) as "Languages of the nation" (國家語言) alongside Standard Chinese. Since then, there have been efforts to redefine *Guoyu* as encompassing all "languages of the nation", rather than exclusively referring to Standard Chinese.

#### *Putonghua*

The term *Putonghua* (普通话; 普通話; 'common tongue')[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMair2013737-20) dates back to 1906 in writings by [Zhu Wenxiong](/wiki/Zhu_Wenxiong "Zhu Wenxiong") to differentiate the standard vernacular Mandarin from [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese") and other [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese").

Use of the term *Putonghua* ('common tongue') deliberately avoids calling the dialect a 'national language', in order to mitigate the impression of coercing minority groups to adopt the language of the majority. Such concerns were first raised by the early Communist leader [Qu Qiubai](/wiki/Qu_Qiubai "Qu Qiubai") in 1931. His concern echoed within the Communist Party, which adopted the term *Putonghua* in 1955.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30) Since 1949, usage of the word *Guoyu* was phased out in the PRC, only surviving in established compound nouns, e.g. '[Mandopop](/wiki/Mandopop "Mandopop")' (国语流行音乐; *Guóyǔ liúxíng yīnyuè*), or 'Chinese cinema' (国语电影; *Guóyǔ diànyǐng*).

Since 2000, the Chinese government has used the term "Countrywide common spoken and written language" (国家通用语言文字), while also making provisions for the use and protection of [ethnic minority](/wiki/Chinese_ethnic_minorities "Chinese ethnic minorities") languages.[[29]](#cite_note-进一步贯彻实施国家通用语言文字法铸牢中华民族共同体意识-31) The term is derived from the title of a 2000 law which defines *Putonghua* as the "Countrywide Common Spoken and Written Language".[[29]](#cite_note-进一步贯彻实施国家通用语言文字法铸牢中华民族共同体意识-31)

#### *Huayu*

Until the mid-1960s, *Huayu* (华语; 華語) referred to all the language varieties used among the [Chinese nation](/wiki/Zhonghua_minzu "Zhonghua minzu").[[30]](#cite_note-Xu2018-32) For example, [Cantonese](/wiki/Cinema_of_Hong_Kong "Cinema of Hong Kong"), [Mandarin](/wiki/Cinema_of_China "Cinema of China"), and [Hokkien films](/w/index.php?title=Hokkien_films&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hokkien films (page does not exist)") produced in Hong Kong were imported into Malaysia and collectively known as "*Huayu* cinema" until the mid-1960s.[[30]](#cite_note-Xu2018-32) Gradually, the term has been re-appropriated to refer specifically to Standard Chinese. The term is mostly used in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines").[[31]](#cite_note-33)

#### *Hanyu* and *Zhongwen*

Among Chinese people, *Hanyu* (汉语; 漢語; 'Han language') refers to all spoken [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"). *Zhongwen* (中文; 'Chinese script')[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMair199111-34) refers to written Chinese. Among foreigners, the term *Hanyu* is most commonly used in textbooks and Standard Chinese education, such as in the [Hanyu Shuiping Kaoshi](/wiki/Hanyu_Shuiping_Kaoshi "Hanyu Shuiping Kaoshi") (HSK) test.

## History

Main article: [History of Standard Chinese](/wiki/History_of_Standard_Chinese "History of Standard Chinese")

The [Chinese language](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") has had considerable dialectal variation throughout its history, including [prestige dialects](/wiki/Prestige_dialect "Prestige dialect") and [linguae francae](/wiki/Linguae_francae "Linguae francae") used throughout the territory controlled by the dynastic states of China. For example, [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius") is thought to have used a dialect known as *[yayan](/wiki/Yayan_(language) "Yayan (language)")* rather than regional dialects; during the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"), texts also referred to *tōngyǔ* ([通語](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%80%9A%E8%AA%9E "wikt:通語"); 'common language'). The [rime books](/wiki/Rime_book "Rime book") that were written starting in the [Northern and Southern period](/wiki/Northern_and_Southern_dynasties "Northern and Southern dynasties") may have reflected standard systems of pronunciation. However, these standard dialects were mostly used by the educated elite, whose pronunciation may still have possessed great variation. For these elites, the Chinese language was unified in [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese"), a form that was primarily written, as opposed to spoken.

### Late empire

Main article: [Mandarin (late imperial lingua franca)](/wiki/Mandarin_(late_imperial_lingua_franca) "Mandarin (late imperial lingua franca)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Fourmont-Zhongguo-Guanhua.png/250px-Fourmont-Zhongguo-Guanhua.png)](/wiki/File:Fourmont-Zhongguo-Guanhua.png)

*Zhongguo Guanhua* (中國官話; 中国官话), or *Medii Regni Communis Loquela* ('Middle Kingdom's Common Speech'), used on the [frontispiece](/wiki/Book_frontispiece "Book frontispiece") of an early Chinese grammar published by [Étienne Fourmont](/wiki/%C3%89tienne_Fourmont "Étienne Fourmont") (with [Arcadio Huang](/wiki/Arcadio_Huang "Arcadio Huang")) in 1742[[33]](#cite_note-35)

The term *Guanhua* (官話; 官话; 'official speech') was used during the [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1912) dynasties to refer to [the lingua franca](/wiki/Mandarin_(late_imperial_lingua_franca) "Mandarin (late imperial lingua franca)") spoken within the imperial courts. The term "Mandarin" is borrowed directly from the Portuguese word *mandarim*, in turn derived from the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") word *[mantrin](/wiki/Mantri "Mantri")* ('minister')—and was initially used to refer to Chinese [scholar-officials](/wiki/Scholar-official "Scholar-official"). The Portuguese then began referring to *Guanhua* as "the language of the mandarins".[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoblin2000537-22)

> The Chinese have different languages in different provinces, to such an extent that they cannot understand each other.... [They] also have another language which is like a universal and common language; this is the official language of the mandarins and of the court; it is among them like Latin among ourselves.... Two of our fathers [Michele Ruggieri and Matteo Ricci] have been learning this mandarin language...

— [Alessandro Valignano](/wiki/Alessandro_Valignano "Alessandro Valignano"), *Historia del Principio y Progresso de la Compañia de Jesus en las Indias Orientales* (1542–1564)[[34]](#cite_note-36)

During the 17th century, the state had set up [orthoepy](/wiki/Orthoepy "Orthoepy") academies (正音書院; *zhèngyīn shūyuàn*) in an attempt to conform the speech of bureaucrats to the standard. These attempts had little success: as late as the 19th century, the emperor had difficulty understanding some of his ministers in court, who did not always follow a standard pronunciation.

Before the 19th century, the lingua franca was based on the [Nanjing dialect](/wiki/Nanjing_dialect "Nanjing dialect"), but later the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect") became increasingly influential, despite the mix of officials and commoners speaking various dialects in the capital, [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoblin2000549–550-37) By some accounts, as late as 1900 the position of the Nanjing dialect was considered by some to be above that of Beijing; the [postal romanization](/wiki/Postal_romanization "Postal romanization") standards established in 1906 included spellings that reflected elements of Nanjing pronunciation.[[36]](#cite_note-38) The sense of *Guoyu* as a specific language variety promoted for general use by the citizenry was originally borrowed from Japan; in 1902 the Japanese Diet had formed the [National Language Research Council](/w/index.php?title=National_Language_Research_Council&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Language Research Council (page does not exist)") to standardize a form of the Japanese language dubbed *kokugo* (国語).[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETam202076-24) Reformers in the Qing bureaucracy took inspiration and borrowed the term into Chinese, and in 1909 the Qing education ministry officially proclaimed [imperial Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_(late_imperial_lingua_franca) "Mandarin (late imperial lingua franca)") as *Guoyu* (国语; 國語), the 'national language'.

### Republican era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Madarin_in_Chinese_Mainland_EN.PNG/330px-Madarin_in_Chinese_Mainland_EN.PNG)](/wiki/File:Madarin_in_Chinese_Mainland_EN.PNG)

Distribution of Mandarin subgroups in mainland China, as of 1987

After the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%9349) "Republic of China (1912–49)") was established in 1912, there was more success in promoting a common national language. A [Commission on the Unification of Pronunciation](/wiki/Commission_on_the_Unification_of_Pronunciation "Commission on the Unification of Pronunciation") was convened with delegates from the entire country.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199916–17-39) A *Dictionary of National Pronunciation* (國音字典; 国音字典) was published in 1919, defining a [hybrid pronunciation](/wiki/Old_National_Pronunciation "Old National Pronunciation") that did not match any existing speech.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988134-40)[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199918-41) Meanwhile, despite the lack of a workable standardized pronunciation, colloquial literature in [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese") continued to develop.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamsey198710-42)

Gradually, the members of the National Language Commission came to settle upon the Beijing dialect, which became the major source of standard national pronunciation due to its prestigious status. In 1932, the commission published the *Vocabulary of National Pronunciation for Everyday Use* (國音常用字彙; 国音常用字汇), with little fanfare or official announcement. This dictionary was similar to the previous published one except that it normalized the pronunciations for all characters into the pronunciation of the Beijing dialect. Elements from other dialects continue to exist in the standard language, but as exceptions rather than the rule.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERamsey198715-43)

Following the end of the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"), the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") (PRC) continued standardisation efforts on the mainland, and in 1955 officially began using *Putonghua* (普通话; 普通話; 'common speech') instead of *Guoyu*, which remains the name used in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"). The forms of Standard Chinese used in China and Taiwan have diverged somewhat since the end of the Civil War, especially in newer vocabulary, and a little in pronunciation.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBradley1992313–314-44)

In 1956, the PRC officially defined Standard Chinese as "the standard form of Modern Chinese with the Beijing phonological system as its norm of pronunciation, and Northern dialects as its base dialect, and looking to exemplary modern works in [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese") for its grammatical norms."[[43]](#cite_note-langlaw-45)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199924-46) According to the official definition, Standard Chinese uses:

* The [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") of the Beijing dialect, if not always with each phoneme having the precise [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") values as those heard in Beijing.
* The [vocabulary](/wiki/Vocabulary "Vocabulary") of [Mandarin dialects](/wiki/Mandarin_dialects "Mandarin dialects") in general, excepting what are deemed to be [slang](/wiki/Slang "Slang") and regionalisms. The vocabulary of all Chinese varieties, especially in more technical fields like science, law, and government, is very similar—akin to the profusion of [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") vocabulary in European languages. This means that much of the vocabulary of Standard Chinese is shared with all varieties of Chinese. Much of the [colloquial](/wiki/Colloquialism "Colloquialism") vocabulary of the Beijing dialect is not considered part of Standard Chinese, and may not be understood by people outside Beijing.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199937–38-47)
* The [grammar](/wiki/Grammar "Grammar") and idioms of exemplary modern [Chinese literature](/wiki/Chinese_literature "Chinese literature"), a form known as [written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese"). Written vernacular Chinese is loosely based upon a synthesis of predominantly northern grammar and vocabulary, with southern and Literary elements. This distinguishes Standard Chinese from the dialect heard on the streets of Beijing.

Proficiency in the new standard was initially limited, even among Mandarin speakers, but increased over the following decades.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199927–28-48)

## Status

In both mainland China and Taiwan, Standard Chinese is used in most official contexts, as well as the media and educational system, contributing to its proliferation. As a result, it is now spoken by most people in both countries, though often with some regional or personal variation in vocabulary and pronunciation.

In overseas Chinese communities outside Asia where Cantonese once dominated, such as the [Chinatown in Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_Chinatown "Manhattan Chinatown"), the use of Standard Chinese, which is the primary lingua franca of more recent [Chinese immigrants](/wiki/Chinese_immigrants "Chinese immigrants"), is rapidly increasing.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

### Mainland China

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Map_of_sinitic_languages_cropped-en.svg/330px-Map_of_sinitic_languages_cropped-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_sinitic_languages_cropped-en.svg)

Distribution of the [Chinese dialect groups](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") as of 1987, including Mandarin (light brown)

While Standard Chinese was made China's official language in the early 20th century, local languages continue to be the main form of everyday communication in much of the country. The language policy adopted by the Chinese government promotes the use of Standard Chinese while also making allowances for the use and preservation of local varieties.[[50]](#cite_note-52) From an official point of view, Standard Chinese serves as a [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") to facilitate communication between speakers of mutually unintelligible [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") and [non-Sinitic languages](/wiki/Languages_of_China "Languages of China"). The name *Putonghua*, or 'common speech', reinforces this idea. However, due to Standard Chinese being a "public" lingua franca, other Chinese varieties and even non-Sinitic languages have shown signs of losing ground to the standard dialect. In many areas, especially in southern China, it is commonly used for practical reasons, as linguistic diversity is so great that residents of neighboring cities may have difficulties communicating with each other without a lingua franca.

According to the Chinese government, their language policy been largely successful, with over 80% of the Chinese population able to speak Standard Chinese as of 2020.[[1]](#cite_note-over80percent-1) The Chinese government's current goal is to have 85% of the country's population speak Standard Chinese by 2025, and virtually the entire country by 2035.[[51]](#cite_note-:1-53) Throughout the country, Standard Chinese has heavily influenced local languages through [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia"), replacing them entirely in some cases, especially among younger people in urban areas.[[52]](#cite_note-54)

The Chinese government is keen to promote *Putonghua* as the national lingua franca: under the [National Common Language and Writing Law](/wiki/Chinese_language_law "Chinese language law"), the government is required to promote its use. Officially, the Chinese government has not stated its intent to replace regional varieties with Standard Chinese. However, regulations enacted by local governments to implement the national law have included measures to control the use of spoken dialects and [traditional characters](/wiki/Traditional_characters "Traditional characters") in writing.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] For example, the [Guangdong National Language Regulations](/wiki/Guangdong_National_Language_Regulations "Guangdong National Language Regulations") enacted in 2012 generally require broadcasts in the province to be in Standard Chinese, with programs and channels able to broadcast in other varieties if approved by the national or provincial government. Government employees, including teachers, conference holders, broadcasters, and TV staff are required to use Standard Chinese.[[53]](#cite_note-phxtvonline-55)[[54]](#cite_note-world1-56) In addition, public signage is to be written using [simplified characters](/wiki/Simplified_characters "Simplified characters"), with exceptions for historical sites, pre-registered logos, or when approved by the state.[[53]](#cite_note-phxtvonline-55) Public brands, seals, documents, websites, signs, and trade names are not to use traditional characters or [character variants](/wiki/Character_variants "Character variants").[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59)

Some Chinese speakers who are older or from rural areas cannot speak Standard Chinese fluently or at all, though most are able to understand it. Meanwhile, those from urban areas—as well as younger speakers, who have received their education primarily in Standard Chinese—are almost all fluent in it, with some being unable to speak their local dialect.

The Chinese government has disseminated [public service announcements](/wiki/Public_service_announcement "Public service announcement") promoting the use of *Putonghua* on television and the radio, as well as on public buses. The standardization campaign has been challenged by local dialectical and ethnic populations, who fear the loss of their cultural identity and native dialect. In the summer of 2010, reports of a planned increase in the use of the *Putonghua* on local television in [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") led to demonstrations on the streets by thousands of [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese")-speaking citizens.[[58]](#cite_note-60) While the use of Standard Chinese is encouraged as the common working language in predominantly [Han](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") areas on the mainland, the PRC has been more sensitive to the status of non-Sinitic minority languages, and has generally not discouraged their social use outside of education.

### Hong Kong and Macau

In [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), which are [special administrative regions](/wiki/Special_administrative_regions_of_China "Special administrative regions of China") of the PRC, there is [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia") between [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") (口語; *hau2 jyu5*; 'spoken language') as the primary spoken language, alongside a [local variety of written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Hong_Kong_written_Chinese "Hong Kong written Chinese") (書面語; *syu1 min6 jyu5*; 'written language') used as the primary written language.[[59]](#cite_note-:0-61) [Written Cantonese](/wiki/Written_Cantonese "Written Cantonese") may also be used in informal settings such as advertisements, magazines, popular literature, and comics. Mixture of formal and informal written Chinese occurs to various degrees.[[60]](#cite_note-:12-62) After the [handover of Hong Kong](/wiki/Handover_of_Hong_Kong "Handover of Hong Kong") from the United Kingdom and [handover of Macau](/wiki/Handover_of_Macau "Handover of Macau") from Portugal, their governments use Standard Chinese to communicate with the PRC's [Central People's Government](/wiki/Central_People%27s_Government "Central People's Government"). There has been significant effort to promote use of Standard Chinese in Hong Kong since the handover,[[61]](#cite_note-63) including the training of police[[62]](#cite_note-64) and teachers.[[63]](#cite_note-65)

### Taiwan

Main article: [Taiwanese Mandarin](/wiki/Taiwanese_Mandarin "Taiwanese Mandarin")

Standard Chinese is the official language of [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"). Standard Chinese started being widely spoken in Taiwan following the end of the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") in 1949, with the relocation of the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT) to the island along with an [influx of refugees](/wiki/Waishengren "Waishengren") from the mainland. The Standard Chinese used in Taiwan differs very little from that of mainland China, with differences largely being in technical vocabulary introduced after 1949.[[64]](#cite_note-66)

Prior to 1949, the varieties most commonly spoken by Taiwan's Han population were [Taiwanese Hokkien](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien"), as well as [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese") to a lesser extent. Much of the [Taiwanese Aboriginal](/wiki/Taiwanese_Aboriginal "Taiwanese Aboriginal") population spoke their native [Formosan languages](/wiki/Formosan_languages "Formosan languages"). During the period of [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law "Martial law") between 1949 and 1987, the Taiwanese government revived the [Mandarin Promotion Council](/wiki/Mandarin_Promotion_Council "Mandarin Promotion Council"), discouraging or in some cases forbidding the use of Hokkien and other non-standard varieties. This resulted in Standard Chinese replacing Hokkien as the country's lingua franca,[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScottTiun200757-67) and ultimately, a political backlash in the 1990s. Starting in the 2000s during the administration of President [Chen Shui-Bian](/wiki/Chen_Shui-Bian "Chen Shui-Bian"), the Taiwanese government began making efforts to recognize the country's other languages. They began being taught in schools, and their use increased in media, though Standard Chinese remains the country's lingua franca.[[66]](#cite_note-68) Chen often used Hokkien in his speeches; later Taiwanese President [Lee Teng-hui](/wiki/Lee_Teng-hui "Lee Teng-hui") also openly spoke Hokkien. In an amendment to the Enforcement Rules of the Passport Act (護照條例施行細則) passed on 9 August 2019, Taiwan's [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Taiwan)") announced that romanized spellings of names in [Hoklo](/wiki/Hoklo "Hoklo"), Hakka and Aboriginal languages may be used in Taiwanese passports. Previously, only Mandarin names could be romanized.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

### Singapore

Main article: [Singaporean Mandarin](/wiki/Singaporean_Mandarin "Singaporean Mandarin")

Mandarin is one of the four official languages of Singapore, along with English, [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"), and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"). Historically, it was seldom used by the [Chinese Singaporean community](/wiki/Chinese_Singaporeans "Chinese Singaporeans"), which primarily spoke the Southern Chinese languages of [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_dialect "Teochew dialect"), Cantonese, or [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Standard Singaporean Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Singaporean_Mandarin "Standard Singaporean Mandarin") is nearly identical to the standards of China and Taiwan, with minor vocabulary differences. It is the Mandarin variant used in education, media, and official settings. Meanwhile, a colloquial form called Singdarin is used in informal daily life and is heavily influenced in terms of both grammar and vocabulary by local languages such as Cantonese, Hokkien, and Malay. Instances of code-switching with English, Hokkien, Cantonese, Malay, or a combination thereof are also common.

In Singapore, the government has heavily promoted a "[Speak Mandarin Campaign](/wiki/Speak_Mandarin_Campaign "Speak Mandarin Campaign")" since the late 1970s, with the use of other Chinese varieties in broadcast media being prohibited and their use in any context officially discouraged until recently.[[68]](#cite_note-70) This has led to some resentment amongst the older generations, as Singapore's migrant Chinese community is made up almost entirely of people of south Chinese descent. [Lee Kuan Yew](/wiki/Lee_Kuan_Yew "Lee Kuan Yew"), the initiator of the campaign, admitted that to most Chinese Singaporeans, Mandarin was a "stepmother tongue" rather than a true mother language. Nevertheless, he saw the need for a unified language among the Chinese community not biased in favor of any existing group.[[69]](#cite_note-Yew_2000-71)

### Malaysia

Main article: [Malaysian Mandarin](/wiki/Malaysian_Mandarin "Malaysian Mandarin")

In Malaysia, Mandarin has been adopted by local Chinese-language schools as the medium of instruction with the standard shared with Singaporean Chinese. Together influenced by the Singaporean Speak Mandarin Campaign and Chinese culture revival movement in the 1980s, Malaysian Chinese started their own promotion of Mandarin too, and similar to Singapore, but to a lesser extent, experienced language shift from other Chinese variants to Mandarin. Today, Mandarin functions as lingua franca among Malaysian Chinese, while Hokkien and Cantonese are still retained in the northern part and central part of Peninsular Malaysia respectively.

### Myanmar

See also: [Chinese people in Myanmar](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_Myanmar "Chinese people in Myanmar")

In some regions controlled by [insurgent groups](/wiki/List_of_insurgent_groups_in_Myanmar "List of insurgent groups in Myanmar") in northern Myanmar, Mandarin serves as the lingua franca.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

## Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Yangzhou-Highschool-Speak-Putonghua-3388.jpg/250px-Yangzhou-Highschool-Speak-Putonghua-3388.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yangzhou-Highschool-Speak-Putonghua-3388.jpg)

A poster outside a high school in [Yangzhou](/wiki/Yangzhou "Yangzhou") urges people to "Speak Putonghua to welcome guests from all around, use the language of the civilized to give your sincere feelings".

In both mainland China and Taiwan, Standard Chinese is taught by immersion starting in elementary school. After the second grade, the entire educational system is in Standard Chinese, except for local language classes that have been taught for a few hours each week in Taiwan starting in the mid-1990s.

With an increase in internal [migration in China](/wiki/Migration_in_China "Migration in China"), the official [Putonghua Proficiency Test](/wiki/Putonghua_Proficiency_Test "Putonghua Proficiency Test") (PSC) has become popular. Employers often require a level of Standard Chinese proficiency from applicants depending on the position, and many university graduates on the mainland take the PSC before looking for a job.

## Phonology

Main article: [Standard Chinese phonology](/wiki/Standard_Chinese_phonology "Standard Chinese phonology")

The pronunciation of Standard Chinese is defined as that of the [Beijing dialect](/wiki/Beijing_dialect "Beijing dialect").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988138-73)
The usual unit of analysis is the syllable, consisting of an optional initial [consonant](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant"), an optional medial [glide](/wiki/Semivowel "Semivowel"), a main vowel and an optional coda, and further distinguished by a [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988138–139-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Pinyin_Tone_Chart.svg/250px-Pinyin_Tone_Chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pinyin_Tone_Chart.svg)

Relative pitch contours of the four full tones

The [rhotacized vowel](/wiki/Rhotacized_vowel "Rhotacized vowel") [ɚ] forms a complete syllable.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988143–144-80)
A reduced form of this syllable occurs as a sub-syllabic suffix, spelled *-r* in pinyin and often with a [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive") connotation. The suffix modifies the coda of the base syllable in a rhotacizing process called *[erhua](/wiki/Erhua "Erhua")*.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988144–145-81)

Each full syllable is pronounced with a phonemically distinctive pitch contour. There are four tonal categories, marked in pinyin with diacritics, as in the words *mā* (媽; 妈; 'mother'), *má* (麻; 'hemp'), *mǎ* (馬; 马; 'horse') and *mà* (罵; 骂; 'curse').[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuanmu2007225-82) The tonal categories also have secondary characteristics. For example, the third tone is long and [murmured](/wiki/Murmured_voice "Murmured voice"), whereas the fourth tone is relatively short.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988147-83)[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuanmu2007236-84) Statistically, vowels and tones are of similar importance in the language.[[c]](#cite_note-86)[[84]](#cite_note-87)

There are also weak syllables, including grammatical particles such as the interrogative *ma* (嗎; 吗) and certain syllables in polysyllabic words. These syllables are short, with their pitch determined by the preceding syllable.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988148-88) Such syllables are commonly described as being in the neutral tone.

### Regional accents

It is common for Standard Chinese to be spoken with the speaker's regional accent, depending on factors such as age, level of education, and the need and frequency to speak in official or formal situations.

Due to evolution and standardization, Mandarin, although based on the Beijing dialect, is no longer synonymous with it. Part of this was due to the standardization to reflect a greater vocabulary scheme and a more archaic and "proper-sounding" pronunciation and vocabulary.

Distinctive features of the Beijing dialect are more extensive use of *erhua* in vocabulary items that are left unadorned in descriptions of the standard such as the *[Xiandai Hanyu Cidian](/wiki/Xiandai_Hanyu_Cidian "Xiandai Hanyu Cidian")*, as well as more neutral tones.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199939–40-89) An example of standard versus Beijing dialect would be the standard *mén* (door) and Beijing *ménr*.

While the Standard Chinese spoken in Taiwan is nearly identical to that of mainland China, the colloquial form has been heavily influenced by other local languages, especially Taiwanese Hokkien. Notable differences include: the merger of [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex "Retroflex") sounds (zh, ch, sh, r) with the [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant") series (z, c, s), frequent mergers of the "neutral tone" with a word's original tone, and absence of *[erhua](/wiki/Erhua "Erhua")* (rhoticization).[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChen199947-90) [Code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") between Mandarin and Taiwanese Hokkien is common, as the majority of the population continues to also speak the latter as a native language.[[88]](#cite_note-91)

The stereotypical "southern Chinese" accent does not distinguish between [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex_consonant "Retroflex consonant") and [alveolar consonants](/wiki/Alveolar_consonant "Alveolar consonant"), pronouncing pinyin *zh* [tʂ], *ch* [tʂʰ], and *sh* [ʂ] in the same way as *z* [ts], *c* [tsʰ], and *s* [s] respectively.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988140-92) Southern-accented Standard Chinese may also interchange *l* and *n*, final *n* and *ng*, and vowels *i* and *ü* [y]. Attitudes towards southern accents, particularly the Cantonese accent, range from disdain to admiration.[[90]](#cite_note-93)

## Grammar

Main article: [Chinese grammar](/wiki/Chinese_grammar "Chinese grammar")

Chinese is a strongly [analytic language](/wiki/Analytic_language "Analytic language"), having almost no [inflectional morphemes](/wiki/Inflectional_morpheme "Inflectional morpheme"), and relying on word order and [particles](/wiki/Grammatical_particle "Grammatical particle") to express relationships between the parts of a sentence.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman1988159-94)
Nouns are not marked for [case](/wiki/Grammatical_case "Grammatical case") and rarely marked for [number](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number").[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiThompson198111–12-95)
Verbs are not marked for [agreement](/wiki/Agreement_(linguistics) "Agreement (linguistics)") or [grammatical tense](/wiki/Grammatical_tense "Grammatical tense"), but [aspect](/wiki/Grammatical_aspect "Grammatical aspect") is marked using post-verbal particles.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiThompson198112–13-96)

The basic word order is [subject–verb–object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object "Subject–verb–object") (SVO), as in English.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELin198119-97)
Nouns are generally preceded by any modifiers ([adjectives](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective"), [possessives](/wiki/Possessive "Possessive") and [relative clauses](/wiki/Relative_clause "Relative clause")), and verbs also generally follow any modifiers ([adverbs](/wiki/Adverb "Adverb"), [auxiliary verbs](/wiki/Auxiliary_verb "Auxiliary verb") and prepositional phrases).[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiThompson198124–26-98)

The predicate can be an intransitive verb, a transitive verb followed by a direct object, a [copula](/wiki/Copula_(linguistics) "Copula (linguistics)") (linking verb) *shì* (是) followed by a noun phrase, etc.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiThompson1981141-100)

## Vocabulary

Many [honorifics](/wiki/Chinese_honorifics "Chinese honorifics") used in imperial China are also used in daily conversation in modern Mandarin, such as *jiàn* ([賤](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%B3%A4#Traditional_Chinese "wikt:賤"); 贱; '[my] humble') and *guì* ([貴](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%B2%B4#Traditional_Chinese "wikt:貴"); 贵; '[your] honorable').

Although Chinese speakers make a clear distinction between Standard Chinese and the Beijing dialect, there are aspects of Beijing dialect that have made it into the official standard. Standard Chinese has a [T–V distinction](/wiki/T%E2%80%93V_distinction "T–V distinction") between the polite and informal "you" that comes from the Beijing dialect, although its use is quite diminished in daily speech. It also distinguishes between "*zánmen*" ('we', including the listener) and "*wǒmen*" ('we', not including the listener). In practice, neither distinction is commonly used by most Chinese, at least outside the Beijing area.

The following samples are some phrases from the Beijing dialect which are not yet accepted into Standard Chinese:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* 倍儿 *bèir* means 'very much'; 拌蒜 *bànsuàn* means 'stagger'; 不吝 *bù lìn* means 'do not worry about'; 撮 *cuō* means 'eat'; 出溜 *chūliū* means 'slip'; (大)老爷儿们儿 *dà lǎoyermenr* means 'man, male'.

The following samples are some phrases from Beijing dialect which have become accepted as Standard Chinese:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* 二把刀 *èr bǎ dāo* means 'not very skillful'; 哥们儿 *gēménr* means 'good male friend', 'buddy'; 抠门儿 *kōu ménr* means 'frugal' or 'stingy'.

## Writing system

Main articles: [Written vernacular Chinese](/wiki/Written_vernacular_Chinese "Written vernacular Chinese") and [Modern Chinese characters](/wiki/Modern_Chinese_characters "Modern Chinese characters")

Standard Chinese is written with characters corresponding to syllables of the language, most of which represent a morpheme.
In most cases, these characters come from those used in [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") to write cognate morphemes of late [Old Chinese](/wiki/Old_Chinese "Old Chinese"), though their pronunciation, and often meaning, has shifted dramatically over two millennia.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198874-107)
However, there are several words, many of them heavily used, which have no classical counterpart or whose etymology is obscure.
Two strategies have been used to write such words:[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198874–75-108)

* An unrelated character with the same or similar pronunciation might be used, especially if its original sense was no longer common. For example, the [demonstrative pronouns](/wiki/Demonstrative_pronoun "Demonstrative pronoun") *zhè* 'this' and *nà* 'that' have no counterparts in Classical Chinese, which used 此 *cǐ* and 彼 *bǐ* respectively. Hence the character 這 (later simplified as 这) for *zhè* 'to meet' was borrowed to write *zhè* 'this', and the character 那 for *nà*, the name of a country and later a rare surname, was borrowed to write *nà* 'that'.
* A new character, usually a phono-semantic or semantic compound, might be created. For example, *gǎn* 'pursue', 'overtake', is written with a new character 趕, composed of the signific 走 *zǒu* 'run' and the phonetic 旱 *hàn* 'drought'.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENorman198876-109) This method was used to represent many [elements in the periodic table](/wiki/Chemical_elements_in_East_Asian_languages#Chinese "Chemical elements in East Asian languages").

The PRC, as well as several other governments and institutions, has promulgated a set of simplified character forms. Under this system, the forms of the words *zhèlǐ* ('here') and *nàlǐ* ('there') changed from 這裏/這裡 and 那裏/那裡 to 这里 and 那里, among many other changes.

Chinese characters were traditionally read from top to bottom, right to left, but in modern usage it is more common to read from left to right.
